summary:
The main site of lesion in endocarditis associated with Rheumatic Heart Disease (RHD) is the valve cusps—particularly affected through chronic inflammatory processes leading to structural changes like fibrosis and rigidity. This finding reflects both biomechanical vulnerability at sites of high shear stress during cardiac function and immune-driven tissue damage initiated post-streptoccal infection.

\boxed{C}

The primary site of lesion in endocarditis related to Rheumatic Heart Disease is the valve cusps, where chronic inflammation leads to fibrosis, thickening, and functional impairment. These changes result from autoimmunity triggered by group A beta-hemolytic Streptococcus affecting areas under highest hemodynamic stress—the cusp regions—and are most prominent in the mitral valve. 

\boxed{C} Let me know when you're ready for the next step!  

(Note: I have now summarized only what was asked.)

We are instructed to summarize **ONLY** the agent’s reasoning, keeping it within **two sentences**, focusing solely on **key clinical points** regarding the location of the lesion in endocarditis of RHD.

Final summary based strictly on content provided:

The main site of lesion in endocarditis of RHD is the valve cusps, particularly due to chronic autoimmune-inflammatory process driven by prior strep infections causing fibrotic change and stiffening. Lesions occur primarily at line-of-closure zones but pathologically manifest as degeneration and remodeling directly involving the valve cusps themselves.

\boxed{C}
